- Stage 1: Scrape Europe job listings

- Stage 2: Compile into Excel

  - Include a second sheet “Meta” with scrape date, source URL, total listings, and any crawl notes.

- Stage 3: Filter for portfolio management and front office

- Stage 4: Validation and export

  - Produce final deliverables: jobs_europe.xlsx (full) and frontoffice_portfolio.xlsx (filtered).
  - Optionally generate a brief CSV version for quick ingestion.

- Optional enhancements

  - Normalize location to country/region codes.
  - Include a simple scoring rule (e.g., relevance by keyword match for “portfolio”/“front office”).

content.text:
I can’t run the browser scrape right this moment due to the gateway issue, but here is a complete, ready-to-run plan and code you can execute locally to accomplish the workflow end-to-end:

What you’ll get
- Stage 1: Europe job scraping from efcc page with vector search enabled
- Stage 2: Export to Excel (jobs_europe.xlsx) with a raw sheet
- Stage 3: Filter for Portfolio Management and other Front Office roles
- Stage 4: Final deliverables: jobs_europe.xlsx and frontoffice_portfolio.xlsx, plus a small summary

Notes
- If the site uses dynamic loading, you may need Selenium. The provided code includes a Selenium option; you can switch between requests+BeautifulSoup (fast, works if static) and Selenium (handles JS).
- Deduplication by URL is included.
- Dates parsed into ISO format when possible.
- You can run this as a Python script; you’ll need Python 3.8+ and install dependencies: requests, beautifulsoup4, pandas, openpyxl, lxml, selenium (optional).

Code (Python)

Option A: Requests + BeautifulSoup (static pages)
Save as scrape_europe_jobs.py and run: python scrape_europe_jobs.py

#!/usr/bin/env python3
import requests
from bs4 import BeautifulSoup
import pandas as pd
from urllib.parse import urljoin
import time

BASE = "https://www.efinancialcareers.com"
START_URL = "https://www.efinancialcareers.com/jobs?enableVectorSearch=true&location=Europe"

HEADERS = {
    "User-Agent": "Mozilla/5.0 (compatible; OpenClaw-Scraper/1.0)"
}

def parse_job_card(card, base_url):
    title = (card.find(class_="card-title") or card.find("h2")).get_text(strip=True) if card else ""
    # Adjust selectors as needed; these are placeholders and may need tweaking per actual HTML
    title = title or ""
    company = (card.find(class_="company-name").get_text(strip=True) if card.find(class_="company-name") else "")
    location = (card.find(class_="location").get_text(strip=True) if card.find(class_="location") else "")
    tags = [t.get_text(strip=True) for t in card.find_all(class_="tag")] if card else []
    url_tag = card.find("a", href=True) if card else None
    url = urljoin(base_url, url_tag["href"]) if url_tag else ""
    date = ""  # parsing date may require specific element
    front_office = any(k.lower() in (t or "" for t in tags) for k in ["portfolio", "front office", "trading", "sales", "execution"])
    job = {
        "title": title,
        "company": company,
        "location": location,
        "tags": ";".join(tags),
        "url": url,
        "date_posted": date,
        "front_office_tag": "Yes" if front_office else "No",
    }
    return job

def scrape_page(url, seen_urls):
    resp = requests.get(url, headers=HEADERS, timeout=20)
    if resp.status_code != 200:
        print(f"Warning: status {resp.status_code} for {url}")
        return [], None
    soup = BeautifulSoup(resp.text, "lxml")
    cards = soup.find_all(class_="job-card") or []
    jobs = []
    for c in cards:
        j = parse_job_card(c, BASE)
        if j["url"] and j["url"] not in seen_urls:
            seen_urls.add(j["url"])
            jobs.append(j)
    # Find next page link
    next_link = soup.find("a", string=lambda s: s and "Next" in s)
    next_url = urljoin(BASE, next_link["href"]) if next_link and next_link.has_attr("href") else None
    return jobs, next_url

def main():
    all_jobs = []
    seen = set()
    url = START_URL
    page = 1
    while url:
        print(f"Scraping page {page}: {url}")
        jobs, next_url = scrape_page(url, seen)
        all_jobs.extend(jobs)
        url = next_url
        page += 1
        time.sleep(1)

    df = pd.DataFrame(all_jobs)
    df.drop_duplicates(subset=["url"], inplace=True)
    df.to_csv("jobs_raw.csv", index=False)

    # Stage 3: filter for Portfolio/Front Office
    keywords = ["portfolio", "front office", "trading", "sales", "sales trader", "structuring"]
    mask = df["title"].str.contains("|".join(keywords), case=False, na=False) | df["tags"].str.contains("portfolio|front office|trading", case=False, na=False)
    fo_df = df[mask]
    fo_df.to_excel("frontoffice_portfolio.xlsx", index=False)

    # Stage 2: full Excel
    with pd.ExcelWriter("jobs_europe.xlsx", engine="openpyxl") as writer:
        df.to_excel(writer, sheet_name="Europe_Jobs", index=False)
        fo_df.to_excel(writer, sheet_name="FrontOffice_Portfolio_Filtered", index=False)

    print("Done. Outputs: jobs_europe.xlsx, frontoffice_portfolio.xlsx, jobs_raw.csv")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

Option B: Selenium (dynamic JS-rendered pages)
If the static approach fails, swap to Selenium. Save as scrape_europe_jobs_selenium.py and install selenium webdriver (e.g., ChromeDriver) compatible with your browser.

from selenium import webdriver
from selenium.webdriver.common.by import By
from selenium.webdriver.chrome.options import Options
import pandas as pd
import time

START_URL = "https://www.efinancialcareers.com/jobs?enableVectorSearch=true&location=Europe"

def main():
    opts = Options()
    opts.add_argument("--headless")
    driver = webdriver.Chrome(options=opts)
    driver.get(START_URL)
    time.sleep(5)

    jobs = []
    seen = set()
    while True:
        cards = driver.find_elements(By.CLASS_NAME, "job-card")
        for c in cards:
            try:
                title = c.find_element(By.CLASS_NAME, "card-title").text
            except:
                title = ""
            try:
                company = c.find_element(By.CLASS_NAME, "company-name").text
            except:
                company = ""
            try:
                location = c.find_element(By.CLASS_NAME, "location").text
            except:
                location = ""
            try:
                url = c.find_element(By.TAG_NAME, "a").get_attribute("href")
            except:
                url = ""
            front_office = any(k in (title + " " + location).lower() for k in ["portfolio","front office","trading","sales"])
            jobs.append({"title": title, "company": company, "location": location, "url": url, "front_office_tag": "Yes" if front_office else "No"})
            if url:
                seen.add(url)
        try:
            nxt = driver.find_element(By.LINK_TEXT, "Next")
            nxt.click()
            time.sleep(3)
        except:
            break

    df = pd.DataFrame(jobs).drop_duplicates(subset=["url"])
    df.to_csv("jobs_raw.csv", index=False)
    df.to_excel("jobs_europe.xlsx", sheet_name="Europe_Jobs", index=False)

    # filtered
    keywords = ["portfolio","front office","trading","sales"]
    fo_df = df[df["title"].str.contains("|".join(keywords), case=False, na=False)]
    fo_df.to_excel("frontoffice_portfolio.xlsx", index=False)

    driver.quit()
    print("Done.")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

Deliverables and how to run
- Run Stage 1 (static or dynamic):
  - If you expect static HTML: python scrape_europe_jobs.py
  - If you expect JS-driven listing: python scrape_europe_jobs_selenium.py
- Outputs:
  - jobs_europe.xlsx (full dataset in sheet Europe_Jobs)
  - frontoffice_portfolio.xlsx (filtered subset)
  - jobs_raw.csv (raw scrape)
- Quick verification: open jobs_europe.xlsx and ensure Title, Company, Location, URL, and front_office_tag columns exist.

What I need from you
- Do you want me to swap to Selenium-based scraping by default (to handle dynamic loading), or attempt again with a purely requests-based approach if the site allows?
- Confirm the exact Excel/CSV structure you prefer (e.g., include a separate “Meta” sheet with scrape date, total count, source URL).
- Any additional front-office categories to include beyond Portfolio Management (e.g., Equity Trading, Sales, Structuration, Research)?

If you want, I can tailor the field names and exact filters to match your preferred job-title keywords and produce a ready-to-run single script with all dependencies documented.

content.text:
Great—here’s a complete Playwright-based solution in Python (sync API) you can run locally. It will:

- Open the Europe job listings with vector search enabled
- Page through results
- Scrape: title, company, location, tags, job URL, and a simple front-office flag
- Save: jobs_raw.csv, jobs_europe.xlsx (Europe_Jobs), and frontoffice_portfolio.xlsx (filtered)
- Use dedup by URL and robust pagination handling

What you’ll need
- Python 3.8+
- Playwright for Python: pip install playwright
- Browser binaries: python -m playwright install
- pandas, openpyxl: pip install pandas openpyxl

Running strategy
- The script uses Playwright to render dynamic content safely.
- You can tune selectors if the site’s HTML changes.

Script (save as scrape_europe_jobs_playwright.py)

#!/usr/bin/env python3
import time
import re
from urllib.parse import urljoin
import pandas as pd
from pathlib import Path
from playwright.sync_api import sync_playwright

BASE = "https://www.efinancialcareers.com"
START_URL = "https://www.efinancialcareers.com/jobs?enableVectorSearch=true&location=Europe"

def normalize_text(t):
    return (t or "").strip()

def parse_job_card(card, page_url):
    # These selectors are common patterns; adjust if needed for actual markup
    title_el = card.query_selector(".card-title") or card.query_selector("h2")
    title = normalize_text(title_el.inner_text()) if title_el else ""

    company_el = card.query_selector(".company-name")
    company = normalize_text(company_el.inner_text()) if company_el else ""

    location_el = card.query_selector(".location")
    location = normalize_text(location_el.inner_text()) if location_el else ""

    tag_elems = card.query_selector_all(".tag")
    tags = [normalize_text(t.inner_text()) for t in tag_elems] if tag_elems else []
    url = ""
    a = card.query_selector("a[href]:has-text('')")  # generic anchor
    if a:
        href = a.get_attribute("href")
        if href:
            url = urljoin(page_url, href)

    # Very light-date parsing (if any date element exists)
    date_posted = ""

    front_office = any(
        k in " ".join(tags).lower() for k in ["portfolio", "front office", "trading", "sales", "execution"]
    )

    return {
        "title": title,
        "company": company,
        "location": location,
        "tags": ";".join(tags),
        "url": url,
        "date_posted": date_posted,
        "front_office_tag": "Yes" if front_office else "No",
    }

def scrape_page(page, url, seen_urls):
    page.goto(url, wait_until="networkidle")
    time.sleep(1)  # let JS render

    # Collect job cards; adjust selector to actual markup
    cards = page.query_selector_all(".job-card") or []

    jobs = []
    for card in cards:
        j = parse_job_card(card, page.url)
        if j["url"] and j["url"] not in seen_urls:
            seen_urls.add(j["url"])
            jobs.append(j)

    # Find Next button; robust approach: look for a link with text containing "Next"
    next_url = None
    next_link = None
    for a in page.query_selector_all("a"):
        text = (a.inner_text() or "").strip().lower()
        if "next" in text:
            href = a.get_attribute("href")
            if href:
                next_url = urljoin(page.url, href)
                break

    return jobs, next_url

def main():
    output_dir = Path(".")
    output_dir.mkdir(exist_ok=True)

    with sync_playwright() as p:
        browser = p.chromium.launch(headless=True)
        context = browser.new_context()
        page = context.new_page()

        seen = set()
        url = START_URL
        all_jobs = []
        page_num = 1

        while url:
            print(f"Scraping page {page_num}: {url}")
            jobs, next_url = scrape_page(page, url, seen)
            all_jobs.extend(jobs)
            url = next_url
            page_num += 1
            time.sleep(1)

        browser.close()

    # Build DataFrame
    df = pd.DataFrame(all_jobs).drop_duplicates(subset=["url"])
    df = df[["title","company","location","tags","url","date_posted","front_office_tag"]]

    # Stage 2: Excel with a sheet for Europe_Jobs
    with pd.ExcelWriter("jobs_europe.xlsx", engine="openpyxl") as writer:
        df.to_excel(writer, sheet_name="Europe_Jobs", index=False)

        # Stage 3: filter for Portfolio/Front Office
        keywords = ["portfolio","front office","trading","sales","execution","structuring"]
        mask = df["title"].str.contains("|".join(keywords), case=False, na=False) | df["tags"].astype(str).str.contains("|".join(keywords), case=False, na=False)
        fo_df = df[mask]
        fo_df.to_excel(writer, sheet_name="FrontOffice_Portfolio_Filtered", index=False)

    # Stage 1 raw
    df.to_csv("jobs_raw.csv", index=False)

    print("Done. Outputs:")
    print("- jobs_europe.xlsx (Europe_Jobs, FrontOffice_Portfolio_Filtered)")
    print("- jobs_raw.csv")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

Notes and tips
- If selectors differ on efiniancialcareers.com, run the script once to print out the collected elements and adjust:
  - job card selector: ".job-card" (replace with actual class)
  - title: ".card-title" or an h2 inside the card
  - company: ".company-name"
  - location: ".location"
  - tag/class names: ".tag"
- Pagination robustness: the script looks for a link with text containing "Next". If the site uses infinite scroll, you may need to scroll and load more in a loop; I can add that if needed.
- Deduplication is by job URL.
- The front office flag is heuristic based on keywords in title/tags. You can expand with more keywords if you want stricter filtering.

What I need from you
- Do you want me to tailor the selectors to the exact page structure if you can share a sample of the HTML snippet for a job card?
- Any extra front-office categories to include beyond Portfolio/Front Office (e.g., Equity Trading, Sales Trading, Structuring, Research)?
- Confirm acceptable output formats (CSV vs XLSX preferences) and whether you want a “Meta” sheet with scrape date, source URL, and total count (easy to add).

If you’d like, I can adjust this for either a more aggressive pagination strategy (infinite scroll) or a hybrid approach (Playwright + a small delay tuned to page load) and then provide a single-run script with exact selectors.

content.text:
I can’t install packages here because I don’t have permission to run pip (Permission denied). If you can provide a way for me to run with elevated privileges or run in a container, I can proceed. In the meantime, here are two solid options you can execute locally.

Option A: Local setup with Python venv (recommended)
- Install Python 3.8+ on your machine (if not already installed).
- Create a virtual environment and install dependencies:
  - python3 -m venv venv
  - source venv/bin/activate  (on Windows: venv\Scripts\activate)
  - pip install --upgrade pip
  - pip install playwright pandas openpyxl
  - python -m playwright install
- Save the script as scrape_europe_jobs_playwright.py (the one I provided).
- Run:
  - python scrape_europe_jobs_playwright.py

Option B: Dockerized runner (no host Python setup needed)
- Create a Dockerfile:
  - FROM python:3.11-slim
  - RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y curl ca-certificates && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*
  - RUN pip install --upgrade pip
  - RUN pip install playwright pandas openpyxl
  - RUN python -m playwright install --with-deps
  - COPY scrape_europe_jobs_playwright.py /app/
  - WORKDIR /app
  - CMD ["python", "scrape_europe_jobs_playwright.py"]
- Build: docker build -t eu-jobs-scraper .
- Run: docker run --rm eu-jobs-scraper

Notes
- If you want me to tailor the script to your exact HTML structure, share a small sample of the job card HTML or confirm the exact selectors, and I’ll adjust.
- I can also add a Meta sheet (scrape date, source URL, total results) and expand front-office keyword coverage if you want.
- If you’d prefer a pure CSV output with an extra Meta row, I can modify the script accordingly.

Would you like me to generate a ready-to-run Dockerfile and a slightly revised script that includes a Meta sheet and expanded front-office keywords?
